summary: Jeff Atwood documents his hands-on experience cleaning up a severe spyware infestation on his Windows XP machine, which he contracted by browsing with an outdated browser to download no-cd patches for games. He sets up a honeypot virtual machine to identify the culprit (GameCopyWorld), then walks through a detailed technical process using Process Explorer and AutoRuns to systematically eliminate the malware. The post serves as both a cautionary tale about browser security and a practical guide for removing stubborn spyware that hooks into system processes like Winlogon and Explorer.
key_points: Browsing with an outdated browser (even briefly) can result in immediate, devastating spyware infection just from visiting a website—no downloads required
A NAT router provides passive protection against infections, but active browsing with unpatched browsers remains extremely dangerous
Spyware removal requires stopping running processes, eliminating autorun entries, and sometimes killing individual threads attached to system processes like Winlogon
Sysinternals tools (Process Explorer and AutoRuns) are essential for identifying and removing malware that standard Windows tools cannot handle
Legitimate-looking websites recommended in community forums can still serve drive-by malware infections to vulnerable browsers
money_quotes: Intentionally choosing to browse the web with a three year old browser, as I did, is an incredibly dangerous thing to do.
Just from visiting the web pages, even if you don't download a single thing – the infestation is nearly immediate and completely devastating.
There should be a special circle of hell reserved for companies who make money doing this to people.
This is my machine, and I'll be damned if I will stand for any slimy, unwanted malware, adware, or spyware that takes up residence on it.
Unless it has a Company Name you recognize, it's highly likely to be a rogue application and should be terminated.
I have a new degree of sympathy for the poor users who become the unfortunate victims of spyware infestations.
We're smarter than the malware vendors.
Any reputable vendor will have no problem attaching their name to their work.
key_insight: Spyware infestations are devastating but defeatable with the right tools and systematic approach—though prevention through keeping browsers updated is far easier than cure.
